one_line_takeaway: TraceGuard reframes reasoning-backdoor detection in LRMs as step-wise sequence labeling and shows that a small (4B) verifier trained with SFT-then-GRPO process supervision -- rather than SFT alone -- avoids 'lexical overfitting' to attack trigger phrases and generalizes far better (e.g. 94.8% vs. 72.8% Post-Hoc Det-F1) to unseen backdoor types, generators, and domains than both content filters (Llama-Guard: 0.0% Det-F1) and self-critique prompting (Chain-of-Scrutiny) at up to 20B parameters.
key_finding: On the primary 334-sample benchmark, Llama-Guard-3-8B scores 0.0% Det-F1 on every reasoning-backdoor category (it only checks output toxicity, which reasoning backdoors don't trigger). Zero-shot baselines also fail badly on unseen attacks: DeepSeek-R1-7B zero-shot gets only 18.0% Det-F1 on Latent Triggers, and GPT-OSS-20B zero-shot drops to 56.0% Det-F1 on Post-Hoc Rationalizations. Chain-of-Scrutiny (self-critique) does well on explicit Fallacy Injections (up to 94.9% Det-F1 at 20B) but collapses on Post-Hoc Rationalizations as scale increases -- Qwen3-4B-CoS gets 51.1%, but 7B and 20B CoS collapse to 23.2% and 22.1% respectively, i.e. larger models are more easily 'persuaded' by fabricated justifications. TraceGuard's Qwen3-4B-Guard achieves 89.7% Det-F1 (Latent Trig.), 99.7% Det-F1 (Fallacy Inj.), and 94.8% Det-F1 (Post-Hoc), with 96.9% Det-Acc on benign inputs -- matching or beating the GPT-OSS-20B-Guard variant in the Post-Hoc category and rivaling the 20B zero-shot/CoS baselines despite being 4B parameters. In the ablation, SSFT alone reaches 99.3% Det-F1 on Fallacy Injections but only 78.9% on Latent Triggers and 72.8% on Post-Hoc; adding VGRL raises these to 89.7% (+10.8%) and 94.8% (+22.0%) respectively, which the authors attribute directly to VGRL correcting the 'lexical overfitting' failure mode. Cross-generator transfer (traces from Gemini-3-Pro, DeepSeek-R1 instead of the GPT-5.2 teacher) stays above 93% Det-F1 in all cases, and cross-domain transfer to GSM8K/CLUTRR stays at 88.6-99.5% Det-F1. Under the adaptive PAIR attack (N=50 query budget), the zero-shot Qwen3-4B baseline's Attack Success Rate rises to 63%, while Qwen3-4B-Guard plateaus at 22% ASR. On efficiency, TraceGuard on an H100 reports 3.4ms TTFT and 293.2 tok/s throughput versus a measured ~720ms TTFT / 42.5 tok/s cloud GPT-5 proxy baseline (6.9x throughput speedup, 211x TTFT reduction), with 1.89GB VRAM in BF16 (~0.76GB at Int4).
core_contribution: TraceGuard is a process-guided verifier ('reasoning firewall') that detects 'reasoning backdoors' in Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) -- cases where a manipulated Chain-of-Thought (CoT) produces a linguistically fluent but logically fallacious justification for a malicious conclusion. The authors formulate detection as step-wise sequence labeling (each reasoning step zt gets a SUPPORTED/UNSUPPORTED validity token) rather than a single global classification, so the verifier must localize the exact 'Point of Fracture' in the trace, not just flag the trace as bad. The pipeline has three stages: (1) an automated forensic-synthesis engine that generates matched contrastive (benign vs. backdoored) reasoning-trace pairs across four adversarial profiles (benign reasoners, fallacy injections, latent backdoors, post-hoc rationalizations); (2) Step-Aware Supervised Fine-Tuning (SSFT) via LoRA that teaches the base model a structural 'verification grammar' (Claim, Verdict) pairs with a selective loss mask; and (3) Verifier-Guided Reinforcement Learning (VGRL) using GRPO with a dense multi-component reward (format integrity, asymmetric verdict accuracy that penalizes false negatives more, set-theoretic step-localization reward, and logical-entailment consistency) designed specifically to eliminate a failure mode they identify and name 'lexical overfitting' -- where an SFT-only verifier learns to associate specific trigger phrases (e.g. 'arcane shove') with the UNSUPPORTED label instead of auditing causal validity.
threat_model: Grey-box: the defender (TraceGuard) sits inside a Trusted Computing Base (TCB) as a local, white-box-accessible security middleware; the Target LRM producing (x, Z, y) is treated as fully untrusted (it may come from an unverified third-party model hub, an opaque API, or unverified fine-tuning data and may harbor supply-chain-injected backdoors). The adversary has query access to the LRM+firewall system and can run adaptive black-box optimization against it (the paper specifically uses PAIR, Prompt Automatic Iterative Refinement) but has no gradient access to the verifier itself and cannot modify the verifier's parameters or training data. Three concrete attack vectors are defined and evaluated: (1) Input-Triggered CoT Manipulation / explicit fallacy injection via a prompt-embedded phrase trigger; (2) Latent Reasoning Backdoors, where the trigger lives in the model's internal reasoning pattern rather than the visible input (supply-chain threat, e.g. a poisoned open-weights 'Legal Contract Analyst' model); (3) Post-Hoc Rationalization, where the model's actual decision comes from an undisclosed shortcut/bias and the CoT is a fabricated, disconnected justification (audit-subversion threat, e.g. discriminatory hiring/compliance screening papered over with a fabricated rationale).
limitations: The paper's own reported weaknesses/gaps: (1) the DeepSeek-R1-7B-Guard variant lags noticeably behind the 4B and 20B TraceGuard variants (e.g. 73.1% Det-F1 on Latent Trig., 71.2% on Fallacy Inj.), which the paper does not explain, suggesting the pipeline's benefit is not monotonic in scale. (2) Under the adaptive PAIR attack, TraceGuard is degraded but not immune -- ASR plateaus at 22%, i.e. roughly 1 in 5 adaptive attacks still succeed against the aligned verifier after 50 iterations. (3) All training/eval data is synthetically generated by the authors' own pipeline using SOTA LLM generators (GPT-5.2 primarily) constrained by JSON schemas, rather than drawn from real-world observed reasoning-backdoor incidents; external validity to naturally occurring attacks is not tested. (4) The defender is granted white-box access to its own verifier and full trust in the local TCB integrity at inference time -- the threat model does not cover compromise of the verifier itself. (5) The grey-box adversary is denied gradient access to the verifier; a stronger white-box or verifier-aware adaptive attacker is not evaluated. (6) Training and evaluation sets are small by modern standards (200 SSFT examples, 800 VGRL examples, 334-sample eval set), and the 'Open Science' repository link given is an anonymized placeholder (anonymous.4open.science), so independent reproduction was not verified by this review.

datasets_used: CLUTRR (VGRL training and cross-domain eval, symbolic logic)
334-sample stratified evaluation benchmark (authors' own, in-distribution latent backdoors + OOD fallacy injections + post-hoc rationalizations)
Source-Variant Benchmark: 300 queries each from Latent Trigger and Post-Hoc sets, re-synthesized with Gemini-3-Pro and DeepSeek-R1
quantitative_results: Llama-Guard-3-8B: 0.0% Det-F1 across all threat categories (Latent Trig., Fallacy Inj., Post-Hoc)
Zero-shot DeepSeek-R1-7B: 18.0% Det-F1 on Latent Triggers
Zero-shot GPT-OSS-20B: 56.0% Det-F1 on Post-Hoc Rationalizations
CoS (Chain-of-Scrutiny) at Qwen3-4B/7B/20B on Post-Hoc: 51.1% / 23.2% / 22.1% Det-F1 (degrades with scale)
CoS GPT-OSS-20B on Fallacy Injections: 94.9% Det-F1
TraceGuard Qwen3-4B-Guard: 89.7% Det-F1 (Latent Trig.), 99.7% (Fallacy Inj.), 94.8% (Post-Hoc), 96.9% Det-Acc (Benign)
Ablation: SSFT-only vs. SSFT+VGRL (Qwen3-4B) on Post-Hoc Det-F1: 72.8% -> 94.8% (+22.0%); on Latent Trig.: 78.9% -> 89.7% (+10.8%); Fallacy Inj. SSFT-only already 99.3%
Cross-generator transfer Det-F1 (Latent/Post-Hoc): GPT-5.2 reference 89.7%/94.8%, Gemini-3-Pro 96.9%/93.8%, DeepSeek-R1 98.8%/100.0%
Cross-domain Det-F1 (Latent/Post-Hoc): CommonSense reference 89.7%/94.8%, GSM8K 95.7%/99.5%, CLUTRR 92.7%/88.6%
PAIR adaptive attack (N=50): zero-shot Qwen3-4B ASR rises to 63%; Qwen3-4B-Guard ASR plateaus at 22%

Efficiency: TraceGuard (H100) TTFT 3.4ms vs. cloud GPT-5 proxy 720ms (211x reduction); throughput 293.2 tok/s vs. 42.5 tok/s (6.9x speedup); VRAM 1.89GB BF16, ~0.76GB Int4; RTX 4090 ~12ms TTFT/~180 tok/s (4.2x); Int4 edge ~45ms TTFT/~40 tok/s (0.9x, i.e. slower than cloud baseline)
baselines_compared: Zero-shot Qwen3-4B, DeepSeek-R1-7B, GPT-OSS-20B (no security alignment)
Chain-of-Scrutiny (CoS) self-critique prompting at Qwen3-4B, DeepSeek-R1-7B, GPT-OSS-20B
GPT-5.2 + Instructions (treated as an upper-bound reference, not a deployable baseline)
